description: Pre-bundled health education articles for offline reading

articles.title: Understanding Malaria: Prevention and Treatment
articles.summary: Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by parasites transmitted through mosquito bites. Early detection and treatment are critical.
articles.content:
## What is Malaria?

Malaria is caused by *Plasmodium* parasites transmitted through the bites of infected female *Anopheles* mosquitoes.

## Symptoms
- High fever with chills
- Sweating and shivering
- Headache and muscle pain
- Nausea and vomiting
- Fatigue

## Prevention
1. **Sleep under insecticide-treated mosquito nets** every night
2. Apply mosquito repellent (DEET-based) to exposed skin
3. Wear long-sleeved clothing, especially at dusk and dawn
4. Eliminate stagnant water sources near your home
5. Use indoor residual spraying if recommended in your area

## Treatment
Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy (ACT) is the first-line treatment. Always complete the full course even if you feel better.

## When to Seek Emergency Care
- Fever > 38.5°C
- Difficulty breathing
- Confusion or seizures
- Unable to keep fluids down

**Do not delay — untreated malaria can be fatal within 24-48 hours.**

articles.title: Diabetes Management for Rural Communities
articles.summary: Practical guidance on managing diabetes with diet, exercise, and medication adherence in resource-limited settings.
articles.content:
## Living with Diabetes

Diabetes requires daily attention but can be managed effectively with the right knowledge.

## Know Your Numbers
- **Normal fasting blood sugar:** 70-100 mg/dL
- **Pre-diabetes:** 100-125 mg/dL
- **Diabetes:** 126 mg/dL or higher (on 2 separate tests)

## Daily Management

### Medication
Take your medication exactly as prescribed. Never skip doses. If you use insulin, keep it stored correctly (2-8°C).

### Diet
- **Eat regularly** — 3 main meals and 2 small snacks
- **Choose complex carbohydrates** — brown rice, whole wheat, oats
- **Include protein** at every meal — lentils, eggs, fish, chicken
- **Limit sugar** — no sugary drinks, sweets, or white bread
- **Eat plenty of vegetables** — they help control blood sugar

### Exercise
A 30-minute walk after meals significantly lowers blood sugar. Start slowly and increase gradually.

### Foot Care
Check your feet every day for cuts, blisters, or redness. Wear proper footwear. See a doctor immediately for any wound.

## Warning Signs

**Low blood sugar (Hypoglycaemia):**
Shaking, sweating, confusion, hunger — drink juice or eat glucose immediately.

**High blood sugar (Hyperglycaemia):**
Excessive thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision — contact your doctor.

articles.title: Maternal and Child Health: A Complete Guide
articles.summary: Essential guidance for pregnant women and new mothers on antenatal care, danger signs, and child vaccination schedules.
articles.content:
## Antenatal Care

Every pregnant woman should have **at least 4 antenatal visits** during pregnancy.

### First Visit (Before 12 weeks)
- Confirm pregnancy and due date
- Blood tests: haemoglobin, blood type, HIV, syphilis
- Start folic acid 400 mcg/day
- Register at local health centre

### Key Danger Signs — Seek Hospital Care Immediately
- **Severe headache** with blurred vision (may indicate pre-eclampsia)
- **Heavy vaginal bleeding** at any time
- **Baby not moving** for 12+ hours after 28 weeks
- **High fever** (infection risk)
- **Swelling** of face, hands, or feet
- Difficulty breathing

## Nutrition During Pregnancy
- **Iron** supplements — prevent anaemia
- **Calcium** — 1000 mg/day for bone development
- **Folic acid** — prevents neural tube defects
- Avoid raw or undercooked meat, unpasteurised milk
- Drink 10-12 glasses of water daily

## Vaccinations Schedule for Children

| Age | Vaccines |
|-----|----------|
| Birth | BCG, OPV-0, Hepatitis B |
| 6 weeks | DPT, IPV, Hib, Rotavirus, PCV |
| 10 weeks | DPT, IPV, Hib, Rotavirus, PCV |
| 14 weeks | DPT, IPV, Hib, Rotavirus, PCV |
| 9 months | Measles-Rubella (MR) |
| 16-24 months | DPT booster, OPV, MR 2nd dose |

articles.title: Clean Water and Sanitation: Preventing Waterborne Diseases
articles.summary: How clean water and proper sanitation protect your family from diarrhea, typhoid, cholera, and other waterborne illnesses.
articles.content:
## Why Clean Water Matters

Waterborne diseases kill hundreds of thousands of people every year. Most cases are entirely preventable.

## How to Make Water Safe

### Boiling
Boil water vigorously for 1 minute. Let cool before drinking. Store in clean covered containers.

### Chlorination
Add 2 drops of household bleach (5% chlorine) per litre of water. Wait 30 minutes before drinking.

### Filtering
Use a clean cloth to filter visible particles before boiling or chlorinating.

## Handwashing — The Single Most Important Habit

Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds:
- Before eating or cooking
- After using the toilet
- After changing diapers
- After handling animals

## Signs of Waterborne Disease

**Diarrhea:**
- Drink ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) immediately
- Make ORS: 1 litre clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + ½ teaspoon salt
- Seek care if blood in stool, high fever, or very young child

**Typhoid:**
- Persistent fever for > 3 days
- Abdominal pain and weakness
- Get tested and treated with antibiotics

articles.title: Mental Health in Rural Areas: Breaking the Stigma
articles.summary: Understanding common mental health conditions and accessible support options for rural communities.
articles.content:
## Mental Health Is Health

One in four people will experience a mental health condition in their lifetime. It is not a sign of weakness.

## Common Conditions

### Depression
**Symptoms:** Persistent sadness, loss of interest, fatigue, sleep changes, feelings of worthlessness

### Anxiety
**Symptoms:** Excessive worry, restlessness, racing heart, difficulty concentrating, sleep problems

### Stress
**Symptoms:** Irritability, headaches, digestive problems, difficulty making decisions

## What Helps

### Self-Care Strategies
- **Talk** to someone you trust
- **Regular sleep** at consistent times
- **Physical activity** — even a 20-minute walk helps
- **Limit alcohol** — it worsens depression
- **Breathing exercises** — 4-7-8 technique: inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec

### When to Seek Professional Help
- Symptoms lasting more than 2 weeks
- Thoughts of self-harm
- Unable to care for yourself or family
- Using substances to cope

## Helplines (India)
- **iCall:** 9152987821
- **Vandrevala Foundation:** 1860-2662-345 (24/7)
- **NIMHANS:** 080-46110007
- **Snehi:** 044-24640050

**You are not alone. Help is available.**
